name: RSC Anderlecht
content:
RSC Anderlecht is a professional football club based in Anderlecht, Brussels, founded in 1908. It competes in the Belgian Pro League and is widely regarded as the most successful club in Belgian football history. Known for its purple and white colors, the club plays its home matches at the Constant Vanden Stock Stadium and has built a strong reputation both domestically and internationally.

Domestically, Anderlecht has dominated Belgian football with a record 34 league titles and multiple Belgian Cup victories. The club established itself as a powerhouse particularly from the mid-20th century onward, including a streak of five consecutive league titles in the 1960s. It has maintained a long-standing presence at the top level of Belgian football since 1935, becoming one of the country’s most consistent and decorated teams.

Internationally, Anderlecht enjoyed its greatest success in the 1970s and 1980s, winning major European trophies such as the UEFA Cup Winners’ Cup (twice), the UEFA Super Cup, and the UEFA Cup. Although its recent years have been less dominant compared to its historical peak, the club remains an important name in European football and continues to develop talent through its strong youth academy.

name: Club Brugge KV
content:
Club Brugge KV is a professional football club based in Bruges, Belgium, founded in 1891. It is one of the most successful and popular clubs in Belgian football, competing in the Belgian Pro League and playing its home matches at the Jan Breydel Stadium. The team is known for its blue and black colors and has a strong fan base, especially in the Flanders region.

Domestically, Club Brugge is the second most decorated club in Belgium, having won numerous league titles, Belgian Cups, and Super Cups. The club experienced major success in the 1970s under coach Ernst Happel, including multiple league championships and consistent top performances. In modern times, it has remained a dominant force, regularly competing for titles and maintaining a strong presence in Belgian football.

Internationally, Club Brugge has a notable European history, reaching the European Cup final in 1978 and the UEFA Cup final in 1976, both times finishing as runner-up. It remains the only Belgian club to have played in a European Cup (Champions League) final and continues to compete in European tournaments, including recent deep runs such as a UEFA Europa Conference League semi-final.

name: R Union Saint-Gilloise
content:
Royale Union Saint-Gilloise is a professional football club from Brussels, Belgium, founded in 1897. It is one of the oldest and historically most successful clubs in Belgian football, playing its home matches at the Joseph Marien Stadium. The club is known for its blue and yellow colors and has deep roots in the capital’s football culture.

Union Saint-Gilloise dominated Belgian football in the early 20th century, winning 11 league titles between 1904 and 1935. During this golden era, the team set a national record by going 60 consecutive league matches unbeaten between 1933 and 1935. After World War II, however, the club declined significantly, eventually falling as low as the fourth tier of Belgian football.

In recent years, the club has experienced a remarkable revival, returning to the top division in 2021 after a 48-year absence. Backed by new ownership, Union quickly became competitive again, consistently challenging for the title and finally winning the Belgian league in 2025, ending a 90-year wait. The club has also re-established itself in European competitions, becoming one of the most notable comeback stories in modern football.

name: R Standard Liège
content:
Standard Liège is a professional football club based in Liège, Belgium, founded in 1898. It is one of the most traditional and historically important clubs in Belgian football, competing in the Belgian Pro League. The club plays its home matches at the Stade Maurice Dufrasne, located in the Sclessin district, and is known for its red kit and passionate fan base.

Throughout its history, Standard has been one of Belgium’s top clubs, winning multiple league titles and domestic cups. It enjoyed strong periods of success in the mid-20th century and again in the late 2000s, when it won back-to-back league championships. The club also holds one of the longest uninterrupted stays in the Belgian top division, having remained there since 1921.

On the European stage, Standard Liège has reached several notable milestones, including a European Cup Winners’ Cup final in 1982, where it finished as runner-up. While not a consistent European powerhouse, the club has produced many talented players and remains an important name in Belgian football, with a strong reputation for its competitive spirit and supporter culture.

name: K Beerschot VAC
content:
K Beerschot VAC was a historic Belgian football club based in Antwerp, founded in 1899. It was one of the early major clubs in Belgian football and became a dominant force in the first half of the 20th century. The club played its home matches at the Olympisch Stadion (Kiel) and was known for its purple and white colors. It was originally formed after players left Antwerp FC, helping establish one of Belgium’s oldest football traditions.

During its golden era, Beerschot won 7 Belgian First Division titles, mainly between the 1920s and 1930s, and also claimed 2 Belgian Cups later in its history. The club was a regular top-flight participant for decades and played an important role in shaping early Belgian football alongside other traditional teams.

Financial problems eventually led to a long decline, and despite several attempts to revive the club identity through mergers and rebranding, the original K Beerschot VAC was dissolved in 1999. Its legacy, however, continued through successor clubs in Antwerp that carried the Beerschot name forward in different forms.

name: R Racing Club de Bruxelles
content:
Royal Racing Club de Bruxelles was founded in 1891 as an athletics club, with its football section established shortly after. It became one of the founding members of both the Royal Belgian Football Association and the early Belgian league structure in 1895. The club enjoyed a dominant early period in Belgian football, winning six league titles within its first two decades and establishing itself as one of the country’s strongest teams. It also won the inaugural Belgian Cup in 1912.

After the interruption of World War I, the club’s performance began to decline. By 1925, RC de Bruxelles was relegated to the second division, marking the start of a long period of instability. Over the following decades, the club moved between the first, second, and even third tiers of Belgian football, but it was never able to return to its former status as a national powerhouse.

In its later years, financial difficulties became increasingly severe and affected both sporting results and long-term stability. The club’s final appearance in the top division came in the 1954–55 season, where it finished near the bottom of the table. Facing ongoing economic problems and discussions about a possible merger with La Rhodienne, the club’s management ultimately agreed to merge in 1963, effectively ending RC de Bruxelles as an independent football institution.

name: R Antwerp FC
content:
Royal Antwerp FC is a professional football club based in Antwerp, Belgium, founded in 1880 and widely known as the oldest club in the country. It is often nicknamed “The Great Old” and plays its home matches at the Bosuilstadion. The club has a long-standing rivalry with Beerschot and is one of the most historically significant teams in Belgian football.

Antwerp has won multiple domestic honors, including five Belgian league titles and four Belgian Cups. After a long period without major success, the club experienced a major revival in the 21st century, returning to the top division in 2017 and quickly re-establishing itself as a competitive force in Belgian football.

In recent years, Antwerp has achieved some of its most important modern successes, including winning the Belgian league title in 2023 and regularly qualifying for European competitions such as the UEFA Champions League. The club’s resurgence has made it one of the strongest teams in Belgium again, combining historic tradition with renewed sporting success.

name: RFC Liège
content:
RFC Liège is a historic Belgian football club based in the city of Liège, founded in 1892. It is one of the oldest clubs in Belgium and holds matricule number 4, meaning it was among the first clubs registered with the Belgian Football Association. The club is traditionally known as “Les Sang et Marine” (The Blood and Navy) and plays its home matches at Stade de Rocourt.

The club is famous for being the first-ever Belgian champion, winning the inaugural national title in 1896, and it has won a total of five Belgian league titles in its history. Its strongest periods came in the early years and again in the 1950s, when it secured back-to-back championships in 1952 and 1953. RFC Liège is also historically significant for its long presence in Belgian national competitions and its strong tradition of developing local players.

Beyond sporting achievements, RFC Liège is also known in European football history due to its role in the Bosman ruling case in 1990, which led to major changes in transfer rules across Europe. In modern times, the club has experienced financial difficulties and lower-league spells, but it remains an important traditional name in Belgian football and currently competes in the Challenger Pro League.

name: R Daring Club Molenbeek
content:
R Daring Club Molenbeek was a historic Belgian football club based in Molenbeek-Saint-Jean, Brussels. It was originally founded in 1895 as Daring Club de Bruxelles and became one of the earliest clubs registered with the Belgian Football Association, receiving matricule number 2. The club was known for its red and black colors and played an important role in the early development of Belgian football. It was one of the traditional “working-class” clubs in Brussels and had a long-standing rivalry with Union Saint-Gilloise.

The club experienced its golden era in the first half of the 20th century, winning five Belgian First Division titles (1912, 1914, 1921, 1936, 1937) and also claiming domestic cup success. Daring was a strong competitive side during the interwar period and remained a regular top-flight team for decades. It also had a notable presence in early European competitions, although without major continental success.

After World War II, the club gradually declined and changed its name several times before becoming Royal Daring Club MolenbeeK In 1973, it merged with Racing White to form RWDM (Racing White Daring Molenbeek), and the original club ceased to exist as an independent entity. Its legacy continues to be part of Brussels football history, especially through later clubs that carried the RWDM identity forward.

name: KV Mechelen
content:
KV Mechelen is a professional football club based in Mechelen, Belgium, founded in 1904. The club competes in the Belgian Pro League and plays its home matches at the Achter de Kazerne stadium. Known for its yellow and red colors, Mechelen is one of the traditional clubs in Belgian football with a strong local identity and passionate supporters.

Domestically, the club has won four Belgian league titles and two Belgian Cups, with its most successful periods coming in the 1940s and the late 1980s. Although it has not been as consistently dominant as some of Belgium’s biggest clubs, Mechelen has remained a competitive side and an important part of the country’s football landscape.

Internationally, KV Mechelen achieved its greatest success in the late 1980s, winning the European Cup Winners’ Cup in 1988 and the UEFA Super Cup the same year. These achievements made it one of the few Belgian clubs to win major European trophies. Despite ups and downs in later years, the club remains notable for its historic European success and continued presence in top-level Belgian football.

name: KRC Genk
content:
KRC Genk is a professional football club based in Genk, Belgium, founded in 1988 following the merger of Waterschei Thor and KFC Winterslag. The club competes in the Belgian Pro League and plays its home matches at the Cegeka Arena. Known for its blue and white colors, Genk quickly established itself as one of Belgium’s leading clubs after reaching the top division in the mid-1990s.

Domestically, Genk has become one of the most successful Belgian clubs of the modern era, winning multiple league titles (including in 1999, 2002, 2011, and 2019) as well as several Belgian Cups. Since the late 1990s, the club has been a consistent contender in the top division and is widely recognized for its strong youth academy, which has produced many notable players.

On the European stage, Genk has regularly participated in UEFA competitions, including several appearances in the UEFA Champions League group stage. While it has not won a major European trophy, the club has maintained a steady presence in international tournaments and achieved respectable results, reinforcing its status as an important club in Belgian and European football.

name: K Lierse SK
content:
Lierse SK (1906) was a Belgian professional football club based in Lier, founded in 1906. It was one of the traditional clubs in Belgian football and played its home matches at the Herman Vanderpoortenstadion. Known for its yellow and black colors, the club built a strong local identity and spent many decades competing at the highest level of Belgian football.

The club enjoyed several successful periods, particularly in the mid-20th century and later in the 1990s. Lierse won four Belgian league titles (1932, 1942, 1960, 1997) and two Belgian Cups (1969, 1999). It was also notable for its strong youth development and produced several important players for Belgian football, while maintaining a reputation as a competitive but smaller club compared to the country’s biggest teams.

Despite its history, Lierse SK faced financial difficulties in the 21st century and was declared bankrupt in 2018, leading to its dissolution. Shortly afterward, a successor club was formed through a merger and relocation, continuing the legacy in a different form. Although the original club no longer exists, it remains an important part of Belgian football history.

name: Cercle Brugge KSV
content:
Cercle Brugge KSV is a professional football club based in Bruges, Belgium, founded in 1899. It is one of the oldest clubs in Belgian football and competes in the Belgian Pro League. The team plays its home matches at the Jan Breydel Stadium, which it shares with its city rival Club Brugge, and is known for its green and black colors.

The club enjoyed its greatest success in the early 20th century, winning three Belgian league titles (1911, 1927, 1930) and becoming the first Flemish club to win the national championship. Cercle also won the Belgian Cup twice, in 1927 and 1985, and has long been considered an important historic club despite often being overshadowed by its more successful city rival.

In modern times, Cercle Brugge has experienced fluctuations between divisions, including relegation spells and a return to the top flight in 2018 after winning the second division. Since 2017, the club has been owned by AS Monaco, helping stabilize its finances and development. Recently, it has re-emerged as a competitive side, including reaching the knockout stage of a European competition for the first time in the 2024–25 UEFA Conference League.

name: KSK Beveren
content:
KSK Beveren was a Belgian professional football club based in Beveren, East Flanders, founded in 1934 and officially registered in 1935. Known for its yellow and blue colors, the club played its home matches at the Freethiel Stadium and developed a strong local identity despite representing a relatively small municipality.

The club enjoyed its greatest success in the late 1970s and early 1980s, winning two Belgian league titles (1979, 1984) and two Belgian Cups. It became the first champion from East Flanders and was often nicknamed “the little Anderlecht” due to its attractive style of play and competitive performances.

From the late 1990s onward, Beveren faced financial difficulties that led to a decline, culminating in the cessation of its men’s first team in 2010 and a merger-related transformation involving Waasland-Beveren. The original club later reactivated its men’s team in 2022, continuing its legacy in lower divisions, while remaining a notable name in Belgian football history.

name: KAA Gent
content:
KAA Gent is a professional football club based in Ghent, Belgium, with roots dating back to 1864 as a multi-sport association and a football section founded in 1900. The club competes in the Belgian Pro League and plays its home matches at the Planet Group Arena. Known as “The Buffalos,” Gent wears blue and white and has been a consistent presence in Belgium’s top division since the late 1980s.

Domestically, Gent achieved its greatest success in the modern era, winning its first and only Belgian league title in the 2014–15 season. In addition, the club has won multiple Belgian Cups across different periods, reflecting its steady competitiveness despite not being among the most dominant traditional powers. Over time, Gent has developed into a stable top-tier club with regular appearances near the top of the league.

On the European stage, Gent has participated in various UEFA competitions, with its most notable moment coming after its historic league win, which led to its debut in the UEFA Champions League group stage. The club has also had solid runs in other European tournaments, reinforcing its reputation as a competitive side beyond Belgium while continuing to grow in stature in recent decades.

name: RWD Molenbeek
content:
RWD Molenbeek, short for Racing White Daring Molenbeek, was a Belgian football club based in Brussels. Its origins date back to 1909 as White Star Club de Bruxelles, later evolving through mergers, most notably in 1963 (forming Racing White) and in 1973 with Daring Club de Bruxelles, which created the final RWD Molenbeek identity. The club played its home matches at the Edmond Machtens Stadium and became an important team in Brussels football.

The club enjoyed its greatest success in the 1970s, shortly after the 1973 merger. It won the Belgian First Division title in the 1974–75 season and regularly competed in European competitions, reaching the semi-finals of the UEFA Cup in 1976–77. During this period, RWD Molenbeek established itself as one of the strongest teams in Belgium and a notable presence on the European stage.

From the mid-1980s onward, the club declined due to financial and sporting difficulties, eventually going bankrupt in 2002. After its dissolution, several successor projects and fan-led initiatives attempted to revive the RWDM name, keeping its legacy alive in Belgian football despite the original club’s disappearance.

name: K Berchem Sport
content:
K Berchem Sport is a Belgian football club based in the Berchem district of Antwerp, founded in 1906, with its football section officially established in 1908. The club competes in the lower tiers of Belgian football and plays its home matches at the Ludo Coeckstadion. Known for its yellow and black colors, Berchem Sport is one of the traditional clubs in the Antwerp region and holds matricule number 28, reflecting its long history.

The club enjoyed its most successful period in the years following World War II, when it finished as runner-up in the Belgian top division three consecutive times between 1949 and 1951. Although it never won the national title, Berchem Sport was considered one of the strongest teams in Belgium during that era and also achieved multiple promotions from the second division across its history.

After its peak years, the club gradually declined and moved between divisions, eventually settling in the amateur levels. Financial challenges and restructurings also affected its trajectory, but Berchem Sport has survived and maintained its identity. Today, it remains an important historic club in Belgian football, known for its tradition and local support despite no longer competing at the highest level.

name: R Charleroi SC
content:
Royal Charleroi S.C. is a professional football club based in Charleroi, Belgium, founded in 1904. Commonly known as Sporting Charleroi, the club competes in the Belgian Pro League and plays its home matches at the Stade du Pays de Charleroi. The team is معروف for its black and white colors, which earned it the nickname “The Zebras.”

Charleroi has spent much of its history moving between divisions but has been a regular presence in the top tier in recent decades, especially since returning in 2012. The club’s best league performance came in the 1968–69 season when it finished as runner-up, while it has also reached the Belgian Cup final twice, though it has never won a major domestic trophy.

Despite limited silverware, Charleroi is considered a stable and competitive club in Belgian football, known for developing talent and occasionally challenging for European qualification spots. In modern times, it has maintained a solid position in the league and remains an important representative of the Walloon region in Belgian football.
stadium: Stade du Pays de Charleroi, Cap. 15,000

name: KSC Lokeren
content:
KSC Lokeren Oost-Vlaanderen was a Belgian football club based in Lokeren, East Flanders, founded in 1923. It played its home matches at the Daknamstadion and was known for its white, black, and yellow colours. The club spent most of its history in the Belgian top division and became a stable mid-to-upper table team over time.

Its strongest period came in the late 1970s and early 1980s, when Lokeren finished second in the Belgian First Division (1980–81), reached the Belgian Cup final, and made a quarter-final run in the UEFA Cup. Later, the club also achieved notable domestic success by winning the Belgian Cup twice, in 2012 and 2014, marking its main trophy successes.

Despite these achievements, financial problems led to the club’s bankruptcy in 2020. It was subsequently merged with KSV Temse, forming a successor club (Lokeren-Temse), while the original Sporting Lokeren identity effectively ceased to exist.

name: SV Zulte Waregem
content:
SV Zulte Waregem is a Belgian football club based in Waregem, West Flanders, formed in 2001 through a collaboration between Zultse VV and KSV Waregem. The club competes in the Belgian football league system and plays its home matches at the Regenboogstadion, which it inherited from the historic KSV Waregem. It is known for its red and green colours and represents a merger of two local football traditions.

The club rose quickly through the divisions after its formation, reaching the Belgian top flight in the mid-2000s and establishing itself as a stable first-division side for many years. One of its standout achievements was finishing second in the Belgian Pro League in the 2012–13 season, which also led to qualification for European competition, including Champions League qualifying rounds and Europa League group stage appearances.

Zulte Waregem has also won major domestic silverware, including two Belgian Cups (2006 and 2017), marking the peak of its success. However, after a long stay in the top division, the club was relegated in 2023 but later bounced back by winning the Challenger Pro League in 2025, securing a return to the Belgian Pro League.

name: K Sint-Truiden VV
content:
Sint-Truidense VV (commonly STVV) is a Belgian professional football club based in Sint-Truiden, Limburg, founded in 1924. The club is known as “De Kanaries” (The Canaries) due to its yellow and blue colours, and it plays its home matches at the Stayen stadium. It competes in the Belgian Pro League and has been a regular top-flight team for much of its modern history.

The club’s most successful period came in the 1960s under coach Raymond Goethals, when STVV finished as runner-up in the Belgian First Division in the 1965–66 season, which remains its highest league achievement. Over the years, the club has also reached the Belgian Cup final twice and has won multiple promotions from the second division, reflecting a pattern of rising and rebuilding across generations.

In modern times, Sint-Truiden has established itself as a stable mid-table club in Belgium’s top division, with a reputation for developing talent and integrating international players. Since 2017, it has been owned by Japanese company DMM.com, which has influenced its recruitment strategy and increased its global visibility, especially through the signing and development of Japanese players.

name: R Léopold Club
content:
Royal Léopold Football Club is a historic Belgian football club based in Uccle, Brussels, founded in 1893, making it one of the oldest active clubs in the country. It is registered under matricule number 5 and was originally established as Léopold Football Club, named in honor of King Léopold II. The club was among the founding members of Belgian football competitions and participated in the very first national championship in 1895–96.

In its early history, Léopold played at the top level of Belgian football and remained in the highest division until 1912, but it was relegated and never managed to return permanently to the elite. During the early 20th century, it gradually declined from national prominence and eventually moved down through the football pyramid, later spending long periods in lower and provincial divisions.

Despite its sporting decline, the club is notable for its historic importance as one of the founding members of Belgian football structures and one of the oldest surviving clubs in Brussels. It has also undergone several reorganizations and mergers to preserve its matricule number and identity, and today it continues to exist in the lower tiers of Belgian football while maintaining its legacy as a pioneer of the sport in Belgium.

name: ROC de Charleroi
content:
Olympic Charleroi is a Belgian football club based in Charleroi, founded in 1911. It is one of the traditional clubs from the city and holds matricule number 246, making it one of the historic registered clubs in Belgian football. The club is known as “les Dogues” and plays its home matches at the Stade de la Neuville. It has long-standing local rivalry with Sporting Charleroi.

The club experienced its golden era in the 1930s and 1940s, when it rose to the top level of Belgian football. Olympic Charleroi reached its peak in the 1946–47 season, finishing as runners-up in the Belgian First Division, narrowly behind Anderlecht. During this period, it was one of the strongest teams in the country and regularly competed in the top flight.

After World War II, the club gradually declined, spending long periods in lower divisions and facing financial instability. It underwent several name changes, mergers, and restructurings, including a merger with Châtelet-Farciennes in 2019. Despite these challenges, Olympic Charleroi has survived multiple bankruptcies and continues to compete in the Belgian football system, representing a historic but fluctuating presence in the country’s football landscape.

name: KRC Mechelen
content:
KRC Mechelen (Koninklijke Racing Club Mechelen), commonly known as Racing Mechelen, is a historic Belgian football club based in Mechelen, founded in 1904. It is one of the traditional clubs of the city and holds matricule number 24, and it is also known for its long-standing rivalry with KV. Mechelen. The club plays its home matches at the Oscar Vankesbeeckstadion and is nicknamed “Den Racing.”

Historically, Racing Mechelen was one of the stronger Belgian sides in the first half of the 20th century. Its best league finish came in the 1951–52 season when it ended as runner-up in the Belgian First Division. The club also reached the Belgian Cup final in 1954 and had several strong top-tier campaigns in the interwar and post-war periods, often finishing in the upper part of the league table.

After its peak years, the club gradually declined and spent more time in lower divisions. From the 2010s onward, it dropped further down the Belgian football pyramid, even playing in provincial leagues for a period. Despite this decline, Racing Mechelen remains an important historic club in Belgium, especially known for its early contributions to top-flight football and its rivalry with KV Mechelen.

name: K Beringen FC
content:
K Beringen FC was a Belgian football club based in Beringen, Limburg, founded in 1924 under the original name Cercle Sportif Kleine Heide. After joining the Belgian Football Association in 1925, the club quickly progressed through the regional levels and reached the national divisions by the late 1930s. Over time, it became one of the stronger clubs in the Limburg region and established itself as a regular participant in Belgium’s national league system.

The club experienced its golden periods mainly between the 1950s and 1970s, when it spent multiple seasons in the Belgian First Division. Its most successful era came in the 1960s, highlighted by a remarkable second-place finish in 1964, which remains the highest achievement in the club’s history. K Beringen FC was known for its stability at a high competitive level and played more than 700 matches in the top tier across different spells.

The club experienced its golden periods mainly between the 1950s and 1970s, when it spent multiple seasons in the Belgian First Division. Its most successful era came in the 1960s, highlighted by a remarkable second-place finish in 1964, which remains the highest achievement in the club’s history. K Beringen FC was known for its stability at a high competitive level and played more than 700 matches in the top tier across different spells.

name: Beerschot AC
content:
Beerschot AC (Koninklijke Beerschot Antwerpen Club) was a Belgian football club based in Antwerp, formed in 1999 through the merger between Germinal Ekeren and the historic Beerschot club. The merger allowed the new team to continue playing in Belgium’s top division while combining Germinal Ekeren’s sporting position with Beerschot’s traditional identity and stadium at the Kiel. The club was widely seen as a continuation of two separate football traditions rather than a completely new entity.

During its existence, Beerschot AC competed mainly in the Belgian First Division and later the Pro League. The club experienced mixed sporting success but managed to win the Belgian Cup in 2005, one of its most notable achievements. It also had periods of relative stability in the top flight, although it struggled with financial management and long-term sustainability. The team was known for its passionate fan base in Antwerp and its rivalry with local clubs.

In 2013, Beerschot AC went bankrupt after being relegated and losing its professional license due to severe financial problems. The club was officially dissolved shortly after. Following its collapse, a new club structure emerged in Antwerp that continued the football tradition in the city under a different name and organisation, while Beerschot AC itself remained part of Belgian football history as a successor in a long chain of Beerschot-related clubs.

name: RE Mouscron
content:
RE Mouscron (Royal Excelsior Mouscron) was a Belgian football club based in the city of Mouscron in Hainaut. The club was originally formed in 1922 through the merger of local teams and became a recognised member of the Belgian Football Association in the same period. Over the decades, it grew into the main football representative of the city and played most of its home matches at Stade Le Canonnier.

The club reached the Belgian top division at various points in its history and became a stable mid-to-upper level team in national competitions. One of its strongest periods came in the late 1990s and early 2000s, when it regularly competed in the Belgian First Division and even qualified for European competitions such as the UEFA Cup. RE Mouscron was known for its solid organisation and ability to compete despite a relatively modest budget compared to larger Belgian clubs.

However, financial instability became a recurring issue in the 2000s, eventually leading to severe debt problems. In December 2009, the club was declared bankrupt and expelled from competition, effectively ceasing to exist in its original form. Shortly after, a successor club was created through a merger involving another local team, continuing the football tradition in Mouscron under a new structure.

name: RFC Seraing
content:
RFC Seraing was a Belgian football club based in Seraing, in the province of Liège. Founded in 1904 as F.C. Sérésien, the club became one of the long-standing teams in Belgian football and later received the “Royal” title after 25 years of existence. It played its home matches at the Stade du Pairay and was known for its red and black colors, as well as its nickname “Les Métallos.”

For much of its history, Seraing competed across different levels of Belgian football, particularly in the second division between the 1930s and early 1950s. The club frequently moved between divisions in the following decades but achieved its greatest success in the early 1980s, when it reached the top tier for the first time. After several ups and downs, Seraing returned to the highest level in the 1993–94 season, finishing an impressive third place and qualifying for the UEFA Cup, where it was eliminated by Dynamo Moscow.

In 1994, the club adopted the name R.F.C. Seraing, but financial problems soon led to its downfall. In 1996, it merged with Standard Liège, and its official registration number was dissolved, marking the end of the original club’s existence. Although another club later adopted the Seraing name, it is considered a separate entity and not a direct continuation of the historic team founded in 1904.

name: K Waterschei SV Thor
content:
K Waterschei SV Thor Genk was a Belgian football club based in Genk, Limburg, founded in 1919 by mining workers. The club originally played under the name Waterschei’s Sport Vereeniging THOR, where “THOR” was an acronym meaning “Tot Herstel Onzer Rechten” (“to restore our rights”), reflecting its working-class origins. It joined the Belgian Football Association in 1925 and was assigned matricule number 553, becoming one of the traditional clubs of the Limburg mining region.

Sportingly, Waterschei had several important periods in Belgian football. The club reached the top division for the first time in the 1950s and returned as a strong First Division side again between 1978 and 1986. Its greatest successes came in the early 1980s, when it won the Belgian Cup twice (1980 and 1982) and reached the semi-finals of the European Cup Winners’ Cup in the 1982–83 season, famously defeating Paris Saint-Germain along the way.

Despite these achievements, the club was also involved in controversy, including a major match-fixing scandal in the early 1980s that affected Belgian football at the highest level. After struggling financially and being relegated to the second division, Waterschei eventually merged with neighbouring KFC Winterslag in 1988. This merger created KR.C. Genk, effectively ending Waterschei as an independent club but preserving its legacy within modern Belgian football.

name: Sporting Club de Bruxelles
content:
Sporting Club de Bruxelles was a short-lived Belgian football club founded in 1894, originally under the name Ixelles Sporting Club. It was one of the early participants in the development of organised football in Belgium and even took part in the country’s first official championship. The club quickly changed its name to Sporting Club de Bruxelles before the start of the inaugural league season and was among the founding members of Belgian football competition structure.

In sporting terms, the club played in the earliest editions of the Belgian top division during the mid-1890s. It finished mid-table in its first season and achieved a 3rd-place finish in the early championship format. However, the team struggled in its second campaign, suffering heavy defeats and eventually withdrawing from the competition during the 1896–97 season. One of its most notable historical footnotes is being part of the very first generation of competitive clubs in Belgium.

The club disappeared completely in 1897 after only a few years of existence, without any continuation or merger. Despite its brief lifespan, Sporting Club de Bruxelles is remembered as part of the foundational era of Belgian football, helping shape the early structure of the national game alongside other pioneering clubs in Brussels and Liège.

name: KVC Westerlo
content:
KVC Westerlo (Koninklijke Voetbal Club Westerlo) is a Belgian professional football club based in the municipality of Westerlo in the province of Antwerp. It was founded in 1933 and plays its home matches at the stadium Het Kuipje, known for its compact size and strong local atmosphere. The club’s traditional colours are yellow and blue, and it is nicknamed “De Kemphanen” (The Roosters of the Campine region).

Historically, Westerlo spent many decades moving through the lower divisions of Belgian football before establishing itself in the top tier in the late 1990s. Since earning promotion to the First Division in 1997, the club has generally remained at the highest level, with only brief relegations. Its most consistent peak performances include several 6th-place finishes in the early 2000s, marking the club’s strongest league era.

The club’s greatest achievement came in the 2000–01 season when it won the Belgian Cup, which also qualified it for European competitions for the first time. Westerlo has participated in UEFA tournaments on a few occasions but has not advanced far in continental play. Despite its modest size compared to Belgium’s biggest clubs, it is known for stability, solid organisation, and a strong regional identity.

name: KSV Waregem
content:
KSV Waregem, officially Koninklijke Sportvereniging Waregem, was a Belgian football club based in Waregem, West Flanders. It was founded in 1925 and became one of the notable clubs in Belgian football, especially after reaching the national leagues and establishing itself in the top division. The club played its home matches at the Regenboogstadion.

The team experienced its golden era mainly between the 1960s and 1980s, regularly competing in the Belgian First Division. One of its biggest achievements came in 1974 when it won the Belgian Cup, and in 1982 it also secured the Belgian Super Cup. Internationally, KSV Waregem made a strong impression in the 1985–86 UEFA Cup, where it reached the semi-finals.

Financial difficulties eventually led to a decline, and after relegations in the late 1990s, the club was dissolved in 2001. It merged with Zultse VV to form SV Zulte Waregem, which continues to play in Belgian professional football today, carrying on part of KSV Waregem’s legacy.

name: RAA Louviéroise
content:
RAA Louviéroise (Royale Association Athlétique Louviéroise), often called RAAL or simply La Louvière, was a Belgian football club based in La Louvière, Hainaut. It was founded in 1913 and became an established club in Belgian football, playing its home matches at the Stade du Tivoli.

The club spent much of its history moving between divisions, but its peak came in the early 2000s when it reached the Belgian First Division. Its greatest achievement was winning the Belgian Cup in the 2002–03 season, which also qualified them for European competition, including the UEFA Cup where they faced Benfica.

Despite this success, the club was later affected by financial and sporting difficulties, including involvement in a match-fixing scandal. After relegations and licensing issues, RAA Louviéroise was dissolved in 2009 and merged into a new structure, leaving its legacy to later successor clubs in La Louvière.

name: RRC Tournai
content:
RRC Tournaisien (Royal Racing Club Tournaisien) was a Belgian football club based in Tournai, in the Hainaut province. It was founded in 1908 and became one of the traditional clubs of the city, playing its home matches at the Stade Drève de Maire. The club was known by the nickname “les Rats” and carried the matricule number 36 in Belgian football.

Throughout its history, the club fluctuated between divisions, reaching the Belgian First Division for the 1958–59 season after earlier spells in the lower leagues. It had several periods in the second and third tiers of Belgian football, with its strongest era coming in the mid-20th century before gradually declining in later decades.

In 2002, RRC Tournaisien merged with RUS Tournaisienne to form RFC Tournai, which inherited its place in Belgian football. This merger marked the end of the club as an independent entity after nearly a century of existence, but its legacy continues through the modern club in Tournai.

name: KV Oostende
content:
KV Oostende (Koninklijke Voetbalclub Oostende) was a Belgian football club based in Ostend, West Flanders. It was originally founded in 1904 as VG Oostende and later merged in 1981 with AS Oostende to form the modern club. The team played its home matches at the Albertparkstadion, later known as the Versluys Arena, and was commonly nicknamed “De Kustboys”.

The club spent much of its history moving between divisions, but it had several periods in Belgium’s top flight. Its best sporting era came in the 2010s, when it established itself in the Belgian First Division A and even qualified for European competition, including the UEFA Europa League in the 2017–18 season.

Despite this success, KV Oostende faced severe financial problems in the early 2020s, accumulating debt and suffering sporting decline. After relegation and failure to secure a professional license, the club went bankrupt and was officially dissolved in 2024, ending more than a century of football history in Ostend.

name: KV Kortrijk
content:
KV Kortrijk (Koninklijke Voetbalclub Kortrijk) is a Belgian professional football club based in Kortrijk, West Flanders. It was founded in 1901 and in its current form established after a merger in 1971. The club is known as “De Kerels” and plays its home matches at the Guldensporenstadion.

Throughout its history, KV Kortrijk has mostly moved between the top two divisions of Belgian football. It first reached the top flight in the early 20th century, returned more consistently from the 1970s onward, and re-established itself in the Belgian Pro League in 2008. One of its best performances came in the 2009–10 season when it finished 4th after the playoffs.

In more recent years, the club has experienced both mid-table stability and relegation battles. Despite financial and sporting challenges at times, KV Kortrijk remains an established name in Belgian football, supported by a loyal local fan base and a strong regional identity.

name: KFC Lommel SK
content:
SK Lommel (often referring to KFC Lommel SK, the former club) was a Belgian football club from Lommel in the Limburg province. It was founded in 1932 and played its home matches at the Stedelijk Sportstadion. The club was a traditional local team that gradually climbed through the Belgian football league system.

The club reached its peak in the 1990s and early 2000s, when it played several seasons in the Belgian First Division. Its best results included a 5th-place finish in the top tier in 1997 and a strong cup run in 2001, where it reached the Belgian Cup final. SK Lommel was known for being a competitive mid-level first-division side during this period.

However, due to financial problems, the club went bankrupt and was dissolved in 2003. After its disappearance, it was effectively replaced through a series of mergers that eventually led to today’s Lommel SK, which continues football in the city under a new structure and ownership.

name: KSK Tongeren
content:
KSK Tongeren (Koninklijke Sportclub Tongeren) is a Belgian football club based in Tongeren, Limburg. It was founded in 1908 as Cercle Sportif Tongrois and is one of the oldest clubs in the region. The club plays its home matches at the SportOase Eburons Dome and is traditionally associated with the blue and white colours.

The club’s history is marked by several mergers and name changes, most notably the 1969 merger with Patria FC Tongeren, which formed the modern KSK Tongeren structure. During the 1970s and early 1980s, the club experienced its strongest period, regularly competing in the Belgian Second Division and even reaching the First Division, where it played two seasons (1981–1983). It also reached the Belgian Cup final in 1974.

The club’s history is marked by several mergers and name changes, most notably the 1969 merger with Patria FC Tongeren, which formed the modern KSK Tongeren structure. During the 1970s and early 1980s, the club experienced its strongest period, regularly competing in the Belgian Second Division and even reaching the First Division, where it played two seasons (1981–1983). It also reached the Belgian Cup final in 1974.

name: KFC Diest
content:
KFC Diest (Koninklijke Football Club Diest) is a Belgian football club based in the city of Diest, founded in 1909. It is one of the older clubs in Belgium and historically played its home matches at the Stedelijk Sportstadion De Warande. The club is associated with the matricule number 41 and traditionally plays in black and white.

The club had its most successful periods in the 1960s and 1970s, when it competed in the Belgian First Division. It played multiple seasons at the top level, with its strongest performance being a 7th-place finish in the 1963–64 season. During this era, it was a solid mid-table first-division team and also had notable runs in domestic competitions.

After its peak, KFC Diest gradually declined through the Belgian football pyramid due to sporting and financial difficulties, going through several mergers and restructurings. In later years it dropped into the lower amateur and provincial divisions, and the club eventually ceased operations in 2025, ending more than a century of football history in Diest.

name: RCS Verviers
content:
RCS Verviétois (Royal Cercle Sportif Verviétois) was a Belgian football club based in Verviers, Liège province. It was founded in 1896 and is considered one of the oldest clubs in Belgium, carrying the matricule number 8, which reflects its early registration with the Belgian Football Association. The club played its home matches at the Stade Communal de Bielmont and was traditionally known for its green and white colours.

Throughout its long history, RCS Verviétois spent many seasons across all levels of Belgian football. The club reached the Belgian First Division several times in the early and mid-20th century, with its strongest era coming in the 1950s when it competed against top teams like Anderlecht and Standard Liège and even reached the Belgian Cup final in 1956.

After the 1960s, the club gradually declined through the divisions and underwent several mergers and restructurings in an attempt to survive. Despite these efforts, financial problems persisted, and after its final seasons in the lower divisions, RCS Verviétois was dissolved in 2015, ending more than a century of football history in Verviers.

name: K Lyra
content:
K Lyra (Koninklijke Lyra) was a Belgian football club based in Lier, Antwerp province. It was founded in 1909 and registered with the Belgian FA under matricule number 52. The club’s name comes from “Lyra,” the Latin name for Lier, and it traditionally played its home matches in the city centre.

Lyra had a long history in Belgian football, spending many seasons in the top two divisions. Its golden period came in the 1930s and 1940s, when it played in the First Division and achieved strong results, including a 6th-place finish in its debut top-flight season and a Belgian Cup final appearance in 1935. The club also won the Second Division multiple times and was a regular competitor at a high national level.

From the 1960s onward, the club declined and eventually faced financial and sporting difficulties. In 1972, K Lyra merged with local rivals Lierse SK, which led to the disappearance of its original matricule and the end of the historic club. Supporters later created successor clubs to continue the Lyra identity in Belgian football.

name: K Tubantia Borgerhout
content:
KFC Tubantia Borgerhout VK was a Belgian football club based in the Borgerhout district of Antwerp. It was founded in 1915 and was affiliated with the Belgian FA under matricule number 64. The club traditionally played in red and white and was one of the historic lower-league teams in Antwerp football.

KFC Tubantia Borgerhout VK was a Belgian football club based in the Borgerhout district of Antwerp. It was founded in 1915 and was affiliated with the Belgian FA under matricule number 64. The club traditionally played in red and white and was one of the historic lower-league teams in Antwerp football.

The club eventually ceased to exist as an independent entity after merging with another local side, becoming part of the football restructuring in Antwerp. This marked the end of KFC Tubantia Borgerhout VK as a standalone club, but its legacy continues through successor teams in the region.

name: KRC Gent
content:
KRC Gent (Koninklijke Racing Club Gent) is a Belgian football club based in Ghent, East Flanders. It was founded in 1899 and is one of the oldest clubs in Belgian football, carrying matricule number 11. The club originated from the merger of several early Ghent-based teams and has long been part of the city’s football history. It plays its home matches at the PGB Stadion in Oostakker.

Historically, KRC Gent was one of the leading clubs in Ghent during the early decades of Belgian football. It competed in the Belgian First Division during the first half of the 20th century and reached respectable results, including a 5th-place finish in the top flight in the 1920s. The club also reached the Belgian Cup final in 1912 and spent many seasons alternating between the first and second divisions.

From the mid-20th century onward, the club gradually declined and eventually settled into lower divisions. Through a series of mergers and name changes, it maintained its existence but never returned to top-flight football. Today, KRC Gent competes at the lower levels of Belgian football, continuing its long tradition as a historic Ghent club despite its reduced sporting status.
